fix_steps: Identify faulted module — check status LEDs on all modules in the POINT I/O bank, red=fault
Reseat the faulted module — lift latch, remove, re-insert onto base firmly until it clicks
Check 24VDC power to the POINT I/O bank — measure at power supply terminals
Check 1734-AENT adapter status — network LED should be solid green, module status should be green
Disconnect field wiring from the faulted module terminal block — if fault clears, the issue is in the field wiring
Compare module catalog number and firmware to the PLC I/O configuration — must match
If module is dead (no LEDs at all), check the base terminal block for 24VDC — replace module if power is present but no response
faults.field_trick: POINT I/O modules have a removable terminal block base. You can pull the module off the base without disconnecting field wiring. Snap a new module onto the existing base — all wiring stays intact. This makes swaps very fast in the field.

faults.name: Allen-Bradley ControlLogix I/O Module Fault (1756 Series)
faults.description: ControlLogix 1756 series I/O module has faulted. Module may show red status LED or is reported faulted in the PLC I/O tree. Associated I/O channels are invalid.
probable_causes: Module not fully seated in the ControlLogix chassis backplane — partial insertion causes loss of backplane communication
Module internal hardware failure — ASIC, relay, or analog circuitry failed
Field wiring issue — shorted sensor wiring pulling an input channel into fault
Module firmware needs update — incompatible with current controller firmware level
Chassis backplane connector damaged — bent pin or contamination on connector
Module configuration does not match physical module — wrong module type in I/O tree
fix_steps: In RSLogix 5000, check I/O tree for the faulted module — right-click > Properties > General shows fault status
Reseat the module — open retention latches, pull module straight out, re-insert firmly until latches click
Check module status LED — green=OK, red=fault, flashing red=needs update or config error
If input module fault, try disconnecting field wiring — if fault clears, issue is in field wiring (shorted sensor, etc.)
Verify module catalog number matches what is configured in the I/O tree — 1756-IB16 is not 1756-IB32
Update module firmware using ControlFlash if firmware mismatch is indicated
If module is dead after reseat, swap to a different chassis slot to rule out backplane connector issue

faults.name: Allen-Bradley EtherNet/IP Adapter Fault (1756-EN2T / 1756-EN2TR)
faults.description: ControlLogix EtherNet/IP communication module has faulted or lost connections. Remote I/O racks, VFDs, and network devices connected through this module are offline.
probable_causes: Module not fully seated in the ControlLogix chassis backplane
Ethernet port link lost — cable disconnected, switch port down, or cable damaged
Module IP address conflict — another device on the network has the same IP
Module connection table full — too many CIP connections configured through this module
Module firmware crash — needs power cycle to recover
DLR (Device Level Ring) break detected on 1756-EN2TR — ring topology has an open segment
fix_steps: Check module front panel LEDs — Network Status (NS) should be green, Module Status (MS) should be green
Check Ethernet cable connections at both ports — verify link LEDs on module and switch
Reseat the module in the chassis if MS LED is off or red
Verify IP address using module rotary switches or RSLinx — check for conflicts on the network
Power cycle the module by removing and re-inserting it — allows firmware to restart
If using DLR ring topology on EN2TR, check ring status — one cable break is OK (ring becomes linear), two breaks cause isolation
Check connection count in module diagnostics — reduce connections or distribute across multiple EN2T modules if at limit

faults.name: Allen-Bradley PanelView HMI Communication Fault
faults.description: PanelView or PanelView Plus HMI lost communication with the ControlLogix/CompactLogix PLC. Screen displays communication error or tag values show as '####' or 'Comm Err'.
probable_causes: Ethernet cable between HMI and PLC (or network switch) disconnected or damaged
PLC went to fault mode or was taken offline — HMI cannot read tags
HMI application shortcut tag path incorrect after PLC controller name change
Network switch port failure between HMI and PLC
HMI IP address changed or conflicts with another device
PLC EtherNet/IP module overloaded — too many HMI connections competing for resources
fix_steps: Check Ethernet cable at HMI and switch — verify link LED on both ends
Verify PLC is in RUN mode — check keyswitch or RSLogix 5000
Ping PLC IP address from HMI terminal settings or from a laptop on the same network
In FactoryTalk View ME/SE, check RSLinx Enterprise shortcut paths — controller name must match PLC
Restart the HMI application — press Ctrl+Alt+Del on PanelView Plus to access Windows desktop if needed
If multiple HMIs on the network, check for IP conflicts — each must have a unique IP
Power cycle the HMI as last resort — hold power button or cycle the 24VDC supply

faults.name: Allen-Bradley Flex I/O Module Fault (1794 Series)
faults.description: Flex I/O module (1794 series) has faulted. Module may show red status LED. Typically found in remote I/O racks connected via DeviceNet, EtherNet/IP, or ControlNet.
probable_causes: Module not fully seated on the Flex I/O backplane terminal base
Module internal hardware failure
Field wiring fault — shorted or open wiring on an I/O channel
1794-AENT or 1794-ADN adapter lost communication with PLC
24VDC power supply to the Flex I/O bank is low or failed
Module configuration mismatch between physical module and PLC I/O tree
fix_steps: Check module status LED — green=OK, red=fault, off=no power or dead
Reseat the module on its terminal base — push firmly until it locks in place
Check 24VDC power to the Flex I/O bank power supply — measure at terminals
Check adapter module (1794-AENT for EtherNet/IP or 1794-ADN for DeviceNet) status LEDs
If field wiring suspected, disconnect wiring from the faulted module — if fault clears, trace the field wiring
Verify module catalog number matches the PLC I/O configuration
If module is dead, swap with a spare — the terminal base retains all field wiring for fast swap

faults.name: Allen-Bradley Micro800 PLC Fault (Micro820/850)
faults.description: Micro820 or Micro850 controller has faulted or stopped running. Status LED shows red or program execution halted. These compact PLCs are used for standalone machine control.
probable_causes: Program download incomplete or corrupted — program needs re-download from Connected Components Workbench
I/O expansion module fault — plug-in module not responding or misconfigured
24VDC power supply voltage out of range — below 19.2V or above 28.8V
Memory full — program too large for controller memory or data table overflow
Embedded I/O channel fault — wiring short on built-in I/O point
Controller firmware corrupted — needs firmware reload via USB
fix_steps: Check controller status LED — solid green=running, flashing green=program stopped, red=fault
Connect with Connected Components Workbench (CCW) via USB or Ethernet — read the fault status
Verify 24VDC power supply — measure at controller terminals, must be 19.2V to 28.8V
If program fault, re-download the program from CCW — verify project matches the controller
Check all plug-in expansion modules — reseat any modules with red LEDs
If firmware corrupted, use CCW to reload firmware via USB connection
Check field wiring on embedded I/O — disconnect suspect wiring and test

faults.name: PowerFlex VFD Fault — Parameter Error / Factory Reset Needed
faults.description: PowerFlex drive reports parameter error, EEPROM fault, or parameters are at factory defaults after unexpected reset. Drive configuration is lost or corrupted.
probable_causes: Drive EEPROM write failure — parameter storage corrupted by power loss during parameter save
Someone performed a factory reset (Parameter A099 = 1) accidentally or intentionally
Firmware update cleared drive parameters — backup not restored after flashing
Drive replacement — new drive from stock has factory default parameters, not application parameters
Communication adapter pushed factory defaults to drive — configuration mismatch
fix_steps: Check if a parameter backup exists — look for a .pf525 or .pf527 parameter file saved during commissioning
If backup available, download parameters using Connected Components Workbench (CCW) or DriveExplorer
If no backup, retrieve parameter sheet from the original commissioning documents or integrator
At minimum, reprogram: Motor NP Volts (A089), Motor NP Hz (A090), Motor NP Amps (A091), Accel Time (A091), Decel Time (A092)
Set communication parameters to match the PLC configuration — IP address, EtherNet/IP assembly instances
After reprogramming, do Parameter A099 = 2 (Save to EEPROM) to lock in the new parameters
Test motor operation at low speed before running at full speed — verify rotation direction and speed reference source
faults.field_trick: Always keep a backup of every drive's parameters. Save a .pf525 file after commissioning and store it in the panel door pocket or on the plant's engineering PC. A drive swap goes from a 2-hour emergency to a 15-minute swap when parameters are backed up.
